scopeNote: Full lifecycle at a multi-strategy hedge fund / institutional asset manager: idea generation -> research -> peer review -> investment committee -> portfolio construction & sizing -> pre-trade compliance -> execution -> post-trade ops/reconciliation -> independent risk monitoring -> performance attribution -> client/regulatory reporting -> periodic review/exit. Each claim is tagged where possible as REGULATORY (a rule/statute), PRACTICE (widely accepted institutional practice), or JUDGMENT (varies by firm).

departments.name: Investment Research (Analysts / Idea Generation)
departments.mandate: Generate, screen, and rigorously underwrite investment ideas; produce documented, reviewable research that supports every position the firm takes and maintain the living thesis for as long as the position is held.
responsibilities: Systematic idea sourcing: screens, factor signals, corporate events, expert networks, sell-side coverage, alternative data
Deep-dive fundamental/quantitative underwriting: financial models, scenario/valuation work, downside cases
Documenting a falsifiable thesis: catalysts, expected value, invalidation levels, time horizon
Peer review / red-teaming of research before it reaches sizing or committee
Ongoing thesis monitoring and re-underwriting after material news, earnings, or drawdown triggers
Escalating possible MNPI contacts and expert-network interactions to Compliance

workflows.name: Idea sourcing and screening
workflows.description: Analysts run quantitative screens and monitor catalysts, filings, and industry contacts; ideas that pass a quick-kill filter get a one-pager for PM triage.
workflows.inputs: Market data, filings, alternative data, expert-network calls (compliance-chaperoned), sell-side research
workflows.outputs: Idea one-pager / pitch ticket with preliminary thesis and expected edge

workflows.name: Deep-dive research memo
workflows.description: Full underwriting: model build, valuation, scenario analysis, risk/reward, position-structuring options. This is the primary artifact consumed by the sizing/committee stage gate.
workflows.inputs: Approved one-pager, data sets, management/expert access cleared by Compliance
workflows.outputs: Research memo, financial model workbook, catalyst calendar, proposed invalidation levels

workflows.name: Peer review / red-team
workflows.cadence: Per memo, before IC or sizing
workflows.description: A non-originating senior analyst or devil's-advocate reviewer challenges assumptions; disagreements are documented rather than resolved by seniority. PRACTICE: formalization varies widely by firm; pods often substitute PM challenge for formal peer review.
workflows.inputs: Research memo and model
workflows.outputs: Peer-review notes, revised memo, documented dissent

workflows.name: Thesis monitoring and re-underwriting
workflows.cadence: Continuous, with formal quarterly or event-driven review
workflows.description: Positions are re-tested against original invalidation levels and catalysts; stale-thesis positions are flagged for exit review.
workflows.inputs: Position list, P&L, news/earnings, original memo
workflows.outputs: Thesis tracker updates, exit/re-size recommendations, post-mortems on closed positions

controls: MNPI policy and expert-network chaperoning with escalation to Compliance (REGULATORY basis: Advisers Act Section 204A; SEC April 2022 Exams risk alert on MNPI deficiencies)
Research communications and memos retained as required records (REGULATORY: Advisers Act Rule 204-2)
Information barriers between pods where the firm may hold conflicting or wall-crossed information (PRACTICE, firm-size dependent)
Documented idea provenance to defend against insider-trading and cherry-picking allegations (PRACTICE)
departments.independence: embedded (first line; owns and manages investment risk day-to-day)
dependencies: Compliance (clearance of expert calls, restricted list)

handoffs.content: Research memo + model + conviction level + proposed structure and invalidation levels
Presentation deck and memo for ideas above committee thresholds
MNPI escalations, expert-network logs, requests to add names to restricted/watch lists
Restricted-list blocks, wall-crossing decisions, chaperone requirements

regulatoryBasis: Advisers Act s.204A and Rule 204A-1 (code of ethics, MNPI prevention)
Advisers Act Rule 204-2 (books and records, incl. memoranda supporting recommendations)
practiceNotes: JUDGMENT: Degree of formal peer review varies enormously — single-strategy funds and long-only institutions tend to formalize it; multi-strategy pod platforms typically leave challenge to the pod PM and central risk rather than a research QA layer.
PRACTICE: A falsifiable, documented thesis with pre-agreed invalidation levels is the widely cited hallmark of institutional-quality research and the anchor artifact for the later exit/review stage.

departments.name: Portfolio Management & Portfolio Construction (PMs / Pods)
departments.mandate: Convert approved research into sized positions within the mandate, risk budget, and limit framework; own the P&L and the risk-adjusted deployment of allocated capital.
responsibilities: Position sizing and portfolio construction (conviction, liquidity, correlation, factor balance)
Hedging and exposure management (gross/net, factor, sector, region)
Order generation with instructions (limits, urgency, participation constraints)
Staying within allocated capital, drawdown limits, and mandate restrictions
Periodic portfolio review and kill/trim discipline on losing or stale positions
Contributing exposures and forward-looking views to Risk and the Investment Committee

workflows.name: Sizing and construction decision
workflows.description: PM sizes the position against risk budget, liquidity (days-to-liquidate), crowding, and existing factor exposures; sizing rationale is recorded. In multi-strategy platforms this happens inside the pod within centrally imposed limits; in traditional managers it may require IC sign-off above thresholds.
workflows.inputs: Research memo, risk snapshot (factor exposures, VaR contribution), capital allocation, limit headroom
workflows.outputs: Target position, order ticket(s) into OMS, documented sizing rationale

workflows.name: Daily portfolio review
workflows.description: Review P&L, exposures vs limits, upcoming catalysts, margin usage; adjust hedges.
workflows.inputs: Overnight risk report, P&L, positions, margin/financing data
workflows.outputs: Rebalance/hedge orders, notes to risk on intended changes

workflows.name: Periodic position/exit review
workflows.description: Formal review of every position against thesis, invalidation levels, and opportunity cost; exits and post-mortems feed back to research quality metrics.
workflows.inputs: Thesis tracker, attribution data, risk utilization
workflows.outputs: Exit/trim decisions, post-mortem memos

controls: Hard mandate/guideline coding in OMS (concentration, instrument, leverage restrictions) (PRACTICE, contractually REGULATORY where in fund docs/IMAs)

Pod-level drawdown limits with automatic capital cuts (PRACTICE at multi-strategy platforms: e.g., ~5% drawdown triggering capital reduction, ~7.5-10% triggering shutdown — exact numbers are JUDGMENT and vary by firm)

Pre-trade allocation statements when orders are aggregated across funds/accounts (REGULATORY expectation: SEC allocation guidance/no-action practice — allocation decided and documented before order entry, pro-rata on partial fills, never performance- or fee-driven)
Separation of PM order entry from execution and from post-trade booking (PRACTICE)

handoffs.content: Orders with side, size, instrument, limit/urgency instructions, allocation intent across accounts
Outbound: intended exposures and new-instrument requests. Inbound: limit breaches, de-risking instructions, scenario results
Outbound: performance and positioning updates. Inbound: capital allocation, mandate changes, conditions on approvals
Hard blocks/warnings from restricted lists, position-limit and shareholder-disclosure thresholds

regulatoryBasis: Fund offering documents / IMAs as binding mandate restrictions (contractual, enforced via Advisers Act antifraud provisions)
SEC trade aggregation and allocation guidance (fair and equitable allocation; documented pre-trade)
practiceNotes: JUDGMENT: Multi-strategy platforms replace committee-gated sizing with tight, automated central risk limits and near-real-time capital reallocation across pods; traditional asset managers gate sizing through IC approval and model portfolios. Both are defensible operating models — the control just sits in a different place.
PRACTICE: Sizing rationale should be a recorded artifact, both for internal post-mortems and to evidence process to allocators (AIMA DDQ sections on investment process).

departments.name: Investment Committee (Governance Forum)
departments.mandate: Act as the stage gate for significant investment decisions and capital allocation; ensure investing complies with the firm's investment policy, risk appetite, and client mandates. Primarily a governance role, not a portfolio-management role.
responsibilities: Approve/reject/condition new ideas above size or novelty thresholds (where the firm uses security-level ICs)
Allocate and re-allocate capital across strategies/pods
Approve new strategies, markets, instruments (jointly with Risk's new-product process)
Review performance, concentration, and adherence to investment policy
Commission and review post-mortems on significant losses
Maintain the investment policy statement / risk appetite alignment with the board

workflows.name: Idea approval meeting
workflows.description: Analyst/PM presents memo; committee challenges thesis, sizing, liquidity and exit plan; decision and conditions minuted. A quorum and documented dissent are governance best practice.
workflows.inputs: Research memo, peer-review notes, risk pre-read (liquidity, factor impact)
workflows.outputs: Approval/rejection/conditions, IC minutes, approved size band

workflows.name: Capital allocation review
workflows.description: Review pod/strategy risk-adjusted performance, capacity, and correlation; reallocate capital and leverage.
workflows.inputs: Attribution by strategy, risk utilization, drawdown history, capacity analysis
workflows.outputs: Capital allocation decisions, pod limit changes, termination decisions

workflows.name: Post-mortem review
workflows.description: Structured review of losers and thesis breaks; findings feed research standards and limit calibration.
workflows.inputs: Post-mortem memos, attribution, risk reports
workflows.outputs: Documented lessons, process changes, minutes

controls: Written terms of reference: composition, quorum, thresholds requiring approval, delegation matrix (PRACTICE)
Minutes with decisions, conditions, and dissent retained (PRACTICE; also supports Rule 204-2 recordkeeping and regulator/allocator review)
CRO/CCO attendance or observer rights (PRACTICE)
departments.independence: governance body — mixed membership (CIO chair, senior PMs, CRO/CCO as members or observers); not a department. Effectiveness depends on genuine challenge, not headcount.

handoffs.content: Approvals with size bands and conditions; capital allocations; mandated de-risking
Outbound: risk appetite decisions and limit change approvals. Inbound: limit breach reports, stress results, concentration warnings
Investment policy compliance reporting, material incidents

regulatoryBasis: No direct US regulatory requirement for an IC at a private adviser; governance expectations arise indirectly via fiduciary duty, Rule 206(4)-7 supervisory structure, and (UK) FCA SM&CR prescribed responsibilities allocated to named senior managers
practiceNotes: JUDGMENT / MAJOR VARIATION: Multi-strategy pod platforms largely do NOT run security-level investment committees — PMs are autonomous within hard central risk limits, and the 'committee' function is really the capital-allocation and limit-setting process run by the CIO office and central risk. Concentrated single-strategy funds and institutional long-only managers use ICs as true per-idea gates. Any generic operating model must support both patterns.

PRACTICE (allocator-facing): Partners Capital / Cambridge Associates-style guidance stresses that a committee's job is governance and policy compliance, delegation of security selection to accountable individuals, and documented decision records.

departments.name: Trading / Execution
departments.mandate: Execute orders to achieve best execution as a fiduciary; manage market impact, counterparty routing, and commission arrangements; feed execution intelligence back to PMs.
responsibilities: Order handling, algo/venue/broker selection, working orders per PM instructions
Best execution monitoring and transaction cost analysis (TCA)
Approved broker list maintenance and broker performance review
Commission management (CSA/soft dollars within Section 28(e) safe harbor where used)
Locates for short sales; coordination with Treasury on financing-sensitive executions
Error identification and escalation (trade error policy)

workflows.name: Order execution
workflows.description: Orders arrive from OMS post pre-trade compliance; trader selects venue/broker/algo; fills allocated per pre-trade allocation statement; executions written back to OMS and downstream.
workflows.inputs: Compliance-cleared orders, market data, broker/algo suite, locate availability
workflows.outputs: Executions with timestamps, allocation records, order audit trail (Rule 204-2 memoranda of orders)

workflows.name: TCA and best execution review
workflows.cadence: Monthly TCA; quarterly Best Execution Committee
workflows.description: Regular and rigorous review of execution quality vs benchmarks (arrival, VWAP, IS); committee reviews routing, broker performance, conflicts (PFOF-free on buy-side, but affiliated-broker or CSA conflicts), documents conclusions and changes.
workflows.inputs: Fill data, TCA vendor output, broker scorecards, commission budgets
workflows.outputs: Best-ex committee minutes, broker list changes, routing changes, documented rationale

workflows.name: Trade error handling
workflows.description: Errors logged, client made whole per policy, root cause and remediation documented, reported to Compliance.
workflows.inputs: Error reports, P&L impact
workflows.outputs: Error log entries, remediation memos

controls: Best execution obligation (REGULATORY: adviser fiduciary duty under the Advisers Act; FINRA Rule 5310 for broker-dealers; FINRA Notice 21-23 'regular and rigorous' review expectation)
Pre-trade compliance gate in OMS before orders reach the desk (PRACTICE, near-universal)
Order/execution records retained (REGULATORY: Rule 204-2(a)(3) memoranda of orders showing who recommended, who placed, account, terms)
Soft dollar usage within Exchange Act Section 28(e) safe harbor, disclosed in Form ADV (REGULATORY where applicable)
Segregation: traders cannot settle or reconcile their own trades (PRACTICE, fundamental SoD control)
departments.independence: first line; PRACTICE/JUDGMENT split — centralized dealing desk (independent from PMs, better for best-ex evidence and info barriers) vs pod-embedded traders (faster, common at multi-strats). Both models exist at scale.

handoffs.content: Executed trades with full economics, broker, allocations — same-day, straight-through where possible
Inbound: orders and instructions. Outbound: fills, market color, liquidity/impact feedback
Trade errors, unusual executions, best-ex committee materials
Locates and borrow costs inbound; financing-relevant execution plans outbound

regulatoryBasis: Adviser fiduciary duty of best execution (SEC interpretive position; enforcement history)
FINRA Rule 5310 and Regulatory Notice 21-23 (where broker-dealer affiliates execute)
Exchange Act s.28(e) (soft dollar safe harbor)
Advisers Act Rule 204-2 (order memoranda)
practiceNotes: PRACTICE: A formal quarterly Best Execution Committee with documented attendees, materials, conclusions, and routing changes is the accepted evidentiary standard on the buy-side even though no rule prescribes the committee itself.

VARIATION: EU/UK venue-level best-execution reports (MiFID II RTS 27/28) have been rolled back in recent years, but the overarching MiFID II/FCA COBS best-execution obligation and monitoring duty remains — do not confuse the abolition of the reports with the abolition of the duty.

departments.name: Compliance (incl. Pre-trade Compliance & Code of Ethics)
departments.mandate: Second-line function that designs, operates, and tests policies and procedures reasonably designed to prevent, detect, and correct violations of securities laws; operates the pre-trade rule gate; owns the code of ethics and regulatory change management.
responsibilities: Maintain compliance manual and annual review under Rule 206(4)-7
Operate pre-trade compliance rules in the OMS: restricted/watch lists, position limits, ownership-disclosure thresholds (13D/G, HSR, sector regimes), mandate restrictions
Code of ethics: personal trading pre-clearance and reporting, gifts & entertainment, outside business activities
MNPI control: information barriers, wall-crossings, expert network policy and chaperoning
Marketing/advertising review under the SEC Marketing Rule
Regulatory exams, inquiries, and disclosure filings (with Legal)
Surveillance: e-comms, trade surveillance vs restricted list and front-running patterns
Compliance training and attestations

workflows.name: Pre-trade compliance gate
workflows.description: OMS-coded rules check every order against restricted lists, concentration and ownership thresholds, mandate restrictions, short-sale and locate requirements; hard blocks require documented compliance override to release.
workflows.inputs: Order flow, restricted/watch lists, positions, regulatory threshold tables, fund guidelines
workflows.outputs: Cleared/blocked orders, override log, threshold alerts (e.g., approaching 5% for 13D/G)

workflows.name: Restricted and watch list maintenance
workflows.description: Names added on MNPI events, wall-crossings, pending transactions; removals governed by cleansing procedures with documented timing (SEC 2022 MNPI risk alert flagged weak add/remove discipline).
workflows.inputs: MNPI escalations, deal flow, expert-network logs
workflows.outputs: Updated lists pushed to OMS and personal-trading system, audit trail

workflows.name: Personal trading pre-clearance and review
workflows.cadence: Daily pre-clearance; quarterly transaction reports; annual holdings reports
workflows.description: Access persons pre-clear per policy (IPOs and limited offerings pre-clearance is REGULATORY under 204A-1; broader pre-clearance is PRACTICE); Compliance reviews against firm trading and restricted lists.
workflows.inputs: Pre-clearance requests, broker feeds, restricted list
workflows.outputs: Approvals/denials, exception reports, escalations

workflows.name: Annual compliance program review
workflows.description: Review adequacy and effectiveness of policies and their implementation, considering compliance events, business changes, and regulatory change; findings and remediation documented. REGULATORY: Rule 206(4)-7; written documentation of the review is now expected.
workflows.inputs: Testing results, breach register, regulatory developments, business changes
workflows.outputs: Annual review report, remediation plan, updated manual

workflows.name: Marketing material review
workflows.description: Review of pitchbooks, letters, performance presentations against the Marketing Rule (fair and balanced, net performance requirements, substantiation).
workflows.inputs: Draft materials, performance data from Performance team
workflows.outputs: Approved materials with review evidence

controls: Designated CCO empowered with full responsibility and authority (REGULATORY: Rule 206(4)-7)
Written policies reasonably designed to prevent MNPI misuse (REGULATORY: s.204A)
Code of ethics with access-person reporting and IPO/limited-offering pre-clearance (REGULATORY: Rule 204A-1)
Five-year recordkeeping, first two years easily accessible (REGULATORY: Rule 204-2)
Compliance testing calendar and forensic testing (PRACTICE)
departments.independence: fully independent second line; CCO must not be subordinated to the front office. In UK firms, compliance oversight is SMF16 under SM&CR with a personal Duty of Responsibility.

handoffs.content: Real-time order blocks/clears, restricted list state, override decisions
Inbound: MNPI escalations and expert-call logs. Outbound: wall-crossing decisions, chaperone requirements, trading restrictions
Shared ownership of filings calendar (13F/13D/G, Form PF inputs), exam responses
Annual review results, material breaches, regulatory contact reports

regulatoryBasis: Advisers Act Rule 206(4)-7 (compliance program + annual review + CCO)
Advisers Act s.204A and Rule 204A-1 (MNPI, code of ethics)
Advisers Act Rule 204-2 (books and records)
SEC Marketing Rule 206(4)-1
FCA SYSC / SM&CR (UK): compliance oversight function SMF16, prescribed responsibilities allocated to senior managers
Exchange Act ownership reporting regimes (13D/G, 13F) as monitored thresholds
practiceNotes: PRACTICE: The pre-trade gate is the single most important automated control point in the lifecycle; institutional allocators (AIMA DDQ) explicitly probe whether restrictions are systematically coded rather than manually policed.
JUDGMENT: Whether surveillance (e-comms, trade surveillance) sits in Compliance or a separate risk/surveillance team varies by size; at most hedge funds it is Compliance-owned with vendor tooling.

departments.name: Independent Risk Management
departments.mandate: Independently identify, measure, monitor, and escalate market, liquidity, counterparty/credit, and operational risk against a board-approved risk appetite; own the limit framework and its enforcement, separate from the people paid to take risk.
responsibilities: Daily/intraday risk measurement: exposures, VaR, stress/scenario, factor decomposition, Greeks, liquidity (days-to-liquidate)
Limit framework design, monitoring, breach escalation, and (at multi-strats) automatic capital cuts on drawdown breaches
Counterparty risk: exposure aggregation across PBs/ISDA counterparties, wrong-way risk, concentration
Liquidity risk: asset liquidity vs investor terms (gates, notice periods), margin/collateral stress
New product / new market approval process (jointly with Ops, Compliance, Valuation)
Risk input to capital allocation and to Form PF / Annex IV risk metrics
Model risk oversight for risk models (with Quant/Model Validation)

workflows.name: Daily risk run and report
workflows.description: Overnight batch computes exposures, VaR, stress, factor and liquidity metrics per pod/strategy/fund; exceptions vs limits flagged; morning risk meeting reviews.
workflows.inputs: Positions (reconciled), market data, factor models, limit tables
workflows.outputs: Daily risk pack, limit utilization dashboard, exception/breach alerts

workflows.name: Limit breach escalation
workflows.description: Breach classified (passive/active), PM notified with cure window; unresolved breaches escalate to CRO/CIO with documented resolution; hard drawdown breaches at pod shops trigger pre-agreed automatic capital reduction.
workflows.inputs: Breach alerts, limit policy, PM response
workflows.outputs: Breach log with resolution, capital-cut instructions, escalation memos

workflows.name: Stress testing and scenario review
workflows.cadence: Weekly/monthly, plus ad hoc on market events
workflows.description: Historical and hypothetical scenarios, correlated-unwind and crowding stress, margin stress across counterparties.
workflows.inputs: Positions, scenario library, financing terms
workflows.outputs: Stress pack for Risk Committee and IC, recommended limit changes

workflows.name: Counterparty review
workflows.description: Review exposure, collateral, and creditworthiness of PBs, ISDA counterparties, FCMs; concentration and rehypothecation risk.
workflows.inputs: Margin/collateral data from Treasury, counterparty financials, CDS/market signals
workflows.outputs: Counterparty scorecards, exposure limits, diversification actions

workflows.name: Risk committee
workflows.description: CRO-chaired review of top risks, limit framework changes, breaches, liquidity, counterparty, operational risk incidents; minuted.
workflows.inputs: Risk packs, breach log, incident register
workflows.outputs: Minutes, limit/appetite change decisions, escalations to board

controls: Functional and hierarchical separation of risk from portfolio management (REGULATORY in EU: AIFMD Art. 15 requires functionally separate risk management for AIFMs; PRACTICE elsewhere — AIMA/MFA sound practices, three-lines model)
Board/management-approved risk appetite statement and limit delegation (PRACTICE)
Independent data path: risk computes from reconciled positions, not PM spreadsheets (PRACTICE)
Documented breach escalation with cure windows (PRACTICE)
Risk metrics reported to regulators via Form PF (REGULATORY for large hedge fund advisers: quarterly filing within 60 days of quarter-end; current-event reporting for qualifying events)
departments.independence: fully independent (second line). CRO reports to CEO/COO or board risk committee, not to the CIO. JUDGMENT: at small funds risk sometimes reports to the CIO or COO — allocators treat CIO-reporting risk as a weakness.

handoffs.content: Limit utilization, breach notices, de-risking/capital-cut instructions, scenario insights
Risk packs, appetite compliance, concentration and crowding warnings
Risk metrics for Form PF / AIFMD Annex IV / CPO-PQR
Reconciled positions, prices, financing balances as the risk data foundation

regulatoryBasis: AIFMD Article 15 (EU AIFMs: functionally separate risk management, risk limits per fund)
SEC Form PF (risk/exposure reporting for large hedge fund advisers; 2024 amendments expanded reporting; compliance dates extended repeatedly in 2025-2026 — verify current status)
No direct US rule mandates an independent CRO at private advisers — independence is PRACTICE anchored in AIMA/MFA sound practices, IIA three-lines model, and allocator due diligence standards
practiceNotes: PRACTICE (multi-strat specific): central risk is the real-time control plane — factor exposures, sector concentrations, and cross-pod correlation monitored continuously, with capital reallocated toward pods performing within risk budgets. This is the defining operational feature distinguishing pod platforms from traditional managers.
JUDGMENT: VaR-centric vs stress-centric limit frameworks vary by strategy mix; illiquid credit books lean on concentration and liquidity limits over VaR.

departments.name: Middle Office / Trade Operations
departments.mandate: Ensure every executed trade is captured, confirmed, allocated, settled, and reconciled accurately and on time; own the integrity of the position and cash record that every downstream function depends on.
responsibilities: Trade capture, enrichment, and same-day affirmation/confirmation (T+0 affirmation now effectively required by US T+1 settlement)
Settlement management and fail resolution
Daily cash, position, and trade reconciliation vs prime brokers, custodians, FCMs, and fund administrator
Corporate actions processing and elections
OTC lifecycle events (resets, novations, compressions) and margin call processing (with Treasury)
Break investigation and resolution with documented aging

workflows.name: Trade capture and affirmation
workflows.description: Executions flow from OMS/EMS into books; economics matched vs broker confirms via CTM/affirmation platforms; exceptions worked same day (US T+1 settlement compresses the window).
workflows.inputs: Executions, broker confirms, SSIs
workflows.outputs: Affirmed trades, exception queue, settlement instructions

workflows.name: Daily reconciliation
workflows.description: Positions, trades, and cash reconciled against every counterparty and the administrator; breaks classified, aged, and escalated by materiality; recon results feed Risk and Fund Accounting as the certified position set.
workflows.inputs: Internal ledger, PB/custodian/FCM feeds, administrator records
workflows.outputs: Recon reports, break log with aging, certified positions

workflows.name: Corporate actions
workflows.description: Capture announcements, notify PMs of elective events with deadlines, process entitlements, reconcile outturns.
workflows.inputs: Custodian/vendor CA feeds, positions
workflows.outputs: Election instructions, booked entitlements, CA audit trail

workflows.name: Settlement fails management
workflows.description: Monitor fails, chase counterparties, manage buy-in risk and CSDR penalties (EU), report chronic fails to Ops management and best-ex review.
workflows.inputs: Settlement status feeds
workflows.outputs: Fails report, resolution actions

controls: Segregation of duties: no one who executes trades settles or reconciles them (PRACTICE, universal)
Four-eyes on manual bookings and SSI changes (PRACTICE; SSI change control is a key fraud control)
Break aging thresholds with mandatory escalation (PRACTICE)
Trade records retention (REGULATORY: Rule 204-2)
Straight-through processing targets to minimize manual touch (PRACTICE)
departments.independence: segregated from front office (first-line control function / shared service). Frequently partially outsourced to middle-office service providers at small/mid funds (JUDGMENT).

handoffs.content: Certified positions, cash, and trade activity as the basis for P&L and NAV
Reconciled position set (the risk data foundation)
Inbound: executions. Outbound: booking breaks, fails requiring trader action
Margin call substantiation, cash projections, collateral movements

regulatoryBasis: SEC T+1 settlement rule (Exchange Act Rule 15c6-1 as amended, May 2024) driving same-day affirmation
Advisers Act Rule 204-2 (trade records)
EU CSDR settlement discipline (for EU-settling trades)
practiceNotes: PRACTICE: Daily full reconciliation (not weekly) is the institutional standard for hedge funds; real-time/continuous recon is the emerging edge as shadow-NAV expectations grow.
JUDGMENT: Build vs outsource — many sub-$5bn funds outsource middle office to their administrator or a specialist; large multi-strats keep it in-house for control and speed.

departments.name: Fund Accounting, Valuation & Finance (incl. Valuation Committee)
departments.mandate: Produce accurate, independently verifiable books and records, P&L, and NAV; own the valuation policy and its consistent application so asset values are not distorted to the disadvantage of investors; manage the audit and fund financials.
responsibilities: Daily P&L production and sign-off with PMs (economic vs accounting P&L reconciliation)
Month-end close and official NAV with the third-party administrator; shadow NAV maintained in-house
Valuation policy ownership: pricing source hierarchy, fair-value methodology for hard-to-price assets, override governance
Valuation Committee operation (independent of portfolio managers)
Management and incentive fee calculations (incl. equalization/series, high-water marks)
Annual audit management and financial statement preparation (custody rule audit for pooled vehicles)
Expense budgeting, allocation between manager and funds per fund docs

workflows.name: Daily P&L and flash
workflows.description: P&L computed from reconciled positions and prices; flash P&L to PMs and management; economic-to-accounting differences tracked.
workflows.inputs: Certified positions, prices per hierarchy, financing accruals
workflows.outputs: Daily P&L reports, price exception queue

workflows.name: Month-end NAV close
workflows.description: Administrator strikes official NAV; in-house team reconciles shadow books trial-balance-to-trial-balance, resolves differences, reviews fee accruals; sign-off checklist completed before NAV release to investors.
workflows.inputs: Administrator NAV pack, shadow ledger, pricing pack, fee schedules
workflows.outputs: Signed-off NAV, reconciliation file, investor-ready NAV statements

workflows.name: Valuation committee
workflows.cadence: Monthly or quarterly, plus ad hoc for events
workflows.description: Reviews hard-to-value positions (Level 3), stale prices, broker-quote dispersion, all price overrides with documented rationale; membership excludes (or outvotes) position-owning PMs. IOSCO principle: an independent party oversees application of valuation policy; overrides must be reported to and reviewed by that party.
workflows.inputs: Pricing pack, third-party valuation agent reports, override requests
workflows.outputs: Minutes, approved marks, override register, policy exceptions

workflows.name: Annual audit
workflows.description: PCAOB-registered auditor audits fund financials; audited statements distributed to investors (custody rule: within 120 days of fiscal year-end for funds relying on the audit exception).
workflows.inputs: Books and records, valuation support, legal confirmations
workflows.outputs: Audited financial statements, management letter, remediation items

controls: Written valuation policy with pricing hierarchy and documented override governance (REGULATORY-adjacent: IOSCO Principles for the Valuation of Hedge Fund Portfolios (2007); AIFMD Art. 19 requires independent valuation function or external valuer in the EU; PRACTICE elsewhere but universally demanded by allocators)
Third-party administrator strikes official NAV; manager maintains shadow books (PRACTICE, near-universal for institutional hedge funds; also an AIMA-documented transparency expectation)
Fair value measured per ASC 820 / IFRS 13 (REGULATORY for financial reporting)
Custody rule: annual audit + distribution of audited financials, or surprise exam (REGULATORY: Advisers Act Rule 206(4)-2)
Segregation: valuation decisions independent of PMs whose compensation depends on marks (PRACTICE/IOSCO principle)
departments.independence: shared service with independent control elements — the Valuation Committee and administrator relationship must be independent of the front office; CFO-led.

handoffs.content: Final NAVs, fee calculations, capital account statements
Official returns and valuations (the accounting book of record feeding the performance book of record)
Marks and pricing uncertainty flags for risk models
AUM/RAUM, balance sheet, and performance inputs for Form PF, ADV, Annex IV
Certified positions, cash, trade activity

regulatoryBasis: IOSCO Principles for the Valuation of Hedge Fund Portfolios (Final Report, Nov 2007) — nine principles incl. independent oversight and override review
AIFMD Article 19 (EU: functionally independent valuation)
Advisers Act Rule 206(4)-2 (custody rule, audited financials route)
ASC 820 / IFRS 13 fair value measurement
practiceNotes: PRACTICE: Shadow accounting (full parallel books) vs 'NAV-lite' checking is a cost/control tradeoff; institutional allocators increasingly expect full shadow with trial-balance reconciliation at month-end (AIMA journal, admin industry literature).
JUDGMENT: Valuation committee composition varies — CFO chair with CCO, CRO, COO members is common; some firms include the CIO which weakens independence and draws ODD criticism.

departments.name: Treasury, Financing & Collateral Management
departments.mandate: Manage the firm's cash, financing, margin, and counterparty relationships to ensure liquidity is available at acceptable cost, collateral is optimized, and cash movement is protected against fraud.
responsibilities: Daily cash management and wire processing with dual authorization
Margin call validation, disputes, and collateral optimization across PBs/ISDA/FCM relationships
Financing negotiation: PB terms, lock-ups, haircuts, stock borrow, repo
Counterparty diversification and unencumbered cash policy
Liquidity forecasting vs subscriptions/redemptions and margin stress
Excess cash investment per policy (treasuries/MMFs)

workflows.name: Daily cash and wires
workflows.description: Project cash by currency/account; process wires under dual authorization with callback verification for new instructions; sweep excess to approved vehicles.
workflows.inputs: Cash balances, settlement obligations, margin calls, redemption schedule
workflows.outputs: Funded obligations, wire audit trail, cash position report

workflows.name: Margin and collateral management
workflows.description: Validate broker margin calls against internal replication, dispute discrepancies, post/recall collateral optimally, track rehypothecation exposure.
workflows.inputs: Counterparty margin statements, internal margin models, collateral inventory
workflows.outputs: Collateral movements, dispute log, margin efficiency metrics

workflows.name: Financing and counterparty review
workflows.description: Review financing spreads, term commitments, borrow availability, counterparty concentration; renegotiate terms; report to Risk Committee.
workflows.inputs: Financing cost data, balances by counterparty, Risk's counterparty assessments
workflows.outputs: Financing summary, term sheets, diversification actions

controls: Dual (or triple) authorization on all wires; callback verification on SSI changes (PRACTICE — the canonical anti-fraud control; probed in every ODD)
Unencumbered cash / excess margin buffers (PRACTICE, post-2008 lesson)
Counterparty exposure limits set jointly with Risk (PRACTICE)
Custody of fund assets with qualified custodians (REGULATORY: Advisers Act custody rule)
departments.independence: shared service under COO/CFO; segregated from front office for cash movement authority.

handoffs.content: Margin, financing, collateral, and counterparty exposure data for liquidity and counterparty risk monitoring
Financing costs, borrow availability, balance-sheet capacity constraints
Cash activity, financing accruals for P&L/NAV

regulatoryBasis: Advisers Act Rule 206(4)-2 (qualified custodian requirement)
Uncleared margin rules / EMIR-CFTC margin requirements for OTC derivatives (where in scope)
practiceNotes: PRACTICE: Multi-prime architecture (2+ PBs) is the institutional standard for funds above ~$500m to manage counterparty risk and financing competition; single-PB is accepted only at small scale.
JUDGMENT: Treasury as standalone function vs folded into Ops/Finance is size-dependent; multi-strats with heavy derivatives usage run dedicated treasury desks with collateral optimization tooling.

departments.name: Quantitative Research & Model Governance
departments.mandate: Build and maintain the models used across the lifecycle (alpha, risk, pricing, optimization) and ensure model risk is governed: models are inventoried, independently validated, monitored, and used within known limitations.
responsibilities: Alpha/signal research and backtesting infrastructure
Portfolio construction/optimization tooling for PMs
Pricing model development for complex instruments
Model inventory, tiering by materiality, documentation standards
Independent model validation (separate from developers) before deployment and periodically thereafter
Ongoing model performance monitoring, drift detection, and change management

workflows.name: Model development and deployment
workflows.description: Develop, document (assumptions, data lineage, limitations), test out-of-sample, submit to validation before production use; changes version-controlled.
workflows.inputs: Research data, historical market data, development standards
workflows.outputs: Model code, model documentation, validation submission pack

workflows.name: Independent validation
workflows.cadence: Pre-deployment; revalidation on a risk-based cycle (1-3 years) or on material change
workflows.description: Reviewer independent of the development team evaluates conceptual soundness, data, implementation, benchmarking, and limitations; issues findings that must be remediated or formally risk-accepted. Anchored on Fed/OCC model risk guidance (SR 11-7 lineage; replaced April 2026 by revised interagency guidance emphasizing a risk-based, tailored approach).
workflows.inputs: Model documentation, code, test results
workflows.outputs: Validation report, findings register, approval/conditions

workflows.name: Model performance monitoring
workflows.description: Live performance vs backtest expectations, input drift, P&L attribution of model-driven books; kill criteria for decayed signals.
workflows.inputs: Live P&L, model outputs, monitoring thresholds
workflows.outputs: Monitoring dashboards, decay alerts, retirement decisions

controls: Model inventory with ownership and materiality tiering (PRACTICE derived from bank guidance)

Validation independent of development (bank REGULATORY under Fed SR 11-7 lineage / OCC Bulletin 2026-13 revised guidance; buy-side application is PRACTICE/JUDGMENT — increasingly demanded by allocators and by SEC exam staff for quant managers)
Version control, reproducibility, and change approval for production models (PRACTICE)
'Effective challenge' standard: critical review by qualified, incentive-independent parties (guidance language)
departments.independence: development is embedded (first line); validation must be independent of developers (second-line style), even if via a small dedicated team or external reviewers.

handoffs.content: Signals, optimizers, analytics with documented limitations
Risk/pricing models with validation status; model risk reporting
Pricing models for hard-to-value instruments (subject to valuation committee governance)

regulatoryBasis: Fed SR 11-7 / OCC 2011-12 (2011) — bank guidance widely used as the buy-side benchmark; NOTE: replaced by revised interagency (OCC/Fed/FDIC) model risk management guidance issued April 2026 (OCC Bulletin 2026-13; Fed SR letter), which adopts a risk-based, non-prescriptive approach and explicitly scopes out generative/agentic AI as rapidly evolving
No direct SEC rule mandates model validation for advisers; expectations surface via Rule 206(4)-7 (are your P&Ps adequate for your business?) and disclosure accuracy
practiceNotes: JUDGMENT: Full bank-style MRM is overkill for a discretionary fund with a handful of spreadsheets; a quant multi-strat running hundreds of production models needs the full framework. Tier the regime to model materiality — the 2026 revised guidance itself moved in this direction.
PRACTICE: Allocator ODD increasingly asks for the model inventory and validation evidence at systematic managers.

departments.name: Performance Measurement & Attribution
departments.mandate: Independently calculate official returns and decompose them into their sources (allocation, selection, factors, fees) so that management, the IC, and clients can judge whether performance reflects intended skill; maintain composite records where GIPS compliance is claimed.
responsibilities: Official return calculation (gross/net, money- and time-weighted as appropriate) from the accounting book of record
Attribution: Brinson-style allocation/selection for long books, factor-based/risk-based attribution for hedged and systematic books
Benchmark administration and data quality
Composite construction and maintenance, GIPS policies, and annual verification support (if the firm claims compliance)
Performance inputs for marketing materials (net performance per SEC Marketing Rule) and investor reporting
Performance fee/high-water-mark verification with Fund Accounting

workflows.name: Return production
workflows.description: Returns computed from official NAV/accounting data; checked against P&L and flows; anomalies investigated before publication.
workflows.inputs: NAV/capital accounts, cash flows, fee data
workflows.outputs: Official return series (performance book of record)

workflows.name: Attribution analysis
workflows.cadence: Monthly; ad hoc for IC and post-mortems
workflows.description: Brinson allocation/selection for benchmark-relative sleeves; factor-based attribution to separate intended vs unintended exposures for absolute-return books; results reviewed with PMs and IC.
workflows.inputs: Positions, returns, benchmarks, factor models
workflows.outputs: Attribution reports by strategy/pod/PM, intended-vs-unintended exposure analysis

workflows.name: GIPS composite maintenance and verification
workflows.description: All fee-paying discretionary portfolios assigned to composites defined by strategy; GIPS reports maintained; firm-wide verification by independent verifier (verification is best practice, not required, and is firm-wide — it cannot be done per-composite).
workflows.inputs: Portfolio returns, composite definitions, GIPS policies
workflows.outputs: Composite records, GIPS reports, verification report

controls: Performance calculated from reconciled accounting data, independent of front-office estimates (PRACTICE, widely accepted)

GIPS 2020 requirements where compliance is claimed: firm-wide compliance, fair-value valuation, composite completeness (VOLUNTARY STANDARD — CFA Institute; not law, but misclaiming compliance is an antifraud problem)
Marketing Rule: net-of-fees presentation and substantiation for advertised performance (REGULATORY: SEC Rule 206(4)-1)
Documented methodology and recalculation evidence retained (Rule 204-2)
departments.independence: independent of front office (widely accepted practice); organizationally usually within Finance/middle office reporting to CFO/COO.

handoffs.content: Attribution by strategy/pod, skill-vs-factor decomposition for capital allocation decisions
Official performance, attribution commentary inputs, GIPS reports
Performance data with methodology for marketing review

regulatoryBasis: GIPS Standards 2020 for Firms (CFA Institute) — voluntary but the de facto institutional standard for composite performance; separate 2020 standards govern verifiers
SEC Marketing Rule 206(4)-1 (performance advertising)
CFA Institute Research Foundation attribution literature (Bacon) for methodology norms
practiceNotes: VARIATION: GIPS adoption is near-universal at institutional long-only managers competing for mandates, but materially less common at hedge funds (single-fund firms get less benefit from composites). Pod platforms rely instead on internal PM-level attribution for capital allocation.

JUDGMENT: Brinson vs factor-based attribution — Brinson answers 'allocation vs selection' vs a benchmark; factor attribution answers 'which risk premia and was it intended'; multi-strats need the latter as primary.

departments.name: Client Reporting & Investor Relations
departments.mandate: Deliver accurate, timely, fair, and compliant communication to investors — performance, exposures, terms, and flows — and manage the subscription/redemption lifecycle with the administrator; be the controlled interface between investors and the firm.
responsibilities: Monthly estimates and final NAV statements; quarterly letters and exposure reports
DDQ and RFP responses (AIMA DDQ is the industry-standard template)
Side letter register and MFN/side-letter obligation monitoring
Subscription/redemption processing with administrator/transfer agent; gate and notice-period administration
Ad hoc investor queries under fair-treatment and selective-disclosure discipline
Marketing collateral coordination (with Compliance approval)

workflows.name: Monthly investor reporting cycle
workflows.description: Flash estimate early in month; final statements after NAV sign-off; commentary drafted, compliance-reviewed, distributed via portal.
workflows.inputs: Signed-off NAV, performance data, exposure summaries, PM commentary
workflows.outputs: Investor statements, monthly letter/tear sheet, portal updates

workflows.name: DDQ/ODD support
workflows.cadence: Per request; standing DDQ refreshed semi-annually
workflows.description: Maintain a golden-source DDQ (AIMA format); coordinate ODD meetings across Ops, Risk, Compliance, Valuation; track allocator commitments made.
workflows.inputs: Current policies, org charts, service-provider list, performance
workflows.outputs: Completed DDQs, ODD meeting records, commitments register

workflows.name: Flows and side letter administration
workflows.description: Process subs/reds with administrator AML/KYC; verify side-letter terms (fees, transparency, capacity) are honored; report flows to Treasury and Risk for liquidity planning.
workflows.inputs: Investor instructions, administrator confirmations, side letter register
workflows.outputs: Dealing confirmations, updated capital schedule, liquidity forecast inputs

controls: All investor-facing materials compliance-reviewed pre-distribution (REGULATORY hook: Marketing Rule, antifraud; PRACTICE for process)
Fair treatment / selective disclosure policy — no material portfolio information to some investors and not others except per disclosed side-letter terms (PRACTICE + antifraud REGULATORY basis)
Side letter register with obligation tracking (PRACTICE; AIMA guidance)
AML/KYC performed by administrator with manager oversight (REGULATORY in most fund domiciles)
departments.independence: front-adjacent shared service; must be independent enough of marketing pressure to report accurately.

handoffs.content: Subscription/redemption pipeline for liquidity planning
Outbound: materials for review. Inbound: approvals, disclosure constraints
Final NAVs and capital account statements

regulatoryBasis: SEC Marketing Rule 206(4)-1; Form ADV Part 2 disclosure obligations

NOTE: the SEC's 2023 Private Fund Advisers rules (mandated quarterly statements, audits, side-letter disclosure) were vacated by the Fifth Circuit in June 2024 — those specific mandates are NOT current law, but many allocators demand equivalent transparency contractually
AIMA Guide to Sound Practices for Investor Relations
practiceNotes: PRACTICE: The AIMA Illustrative DDQ is the de facto industry standard for manager due diligence; maintaining a current golden-source response set is table stakes for institutional fundraising.
JUDGMENT: Transparency level (full position-level via managed accounts/risk aggregators vs exposure summaries) varies by strategy sensitivity and investor size.

departments.name: Legal & Regulatory Reporting
departments.mandate: Maintain the legal integrity of the fund complex and management company — documents, contracts, filings — and ensure complete, accurate, on-time regulatory reporting across all jurisdictions.
responsibilities: Fund documents (PPM/LPA), amendments, and new vehicle launches
Side letters negotiation (with IR) and counterparty documentation (ISDA/CSA, PB agreements, futures agreements)

Regulatory filings calendar: Form ADV (annual amendment + prompt updates), Form PF (quarterly for large hedge fund advisers, within 60 days of quarter-end; current reports on trigger events), 13F (quarterly), 13D/G (event-driven), Section 16 where applicable, CFTC/NFA CPO-PQR or CTA filings, AIFMD Annex IV, EMIR/Dodd-Frank trade reporting oversight
Litigation, regulatory inquiries and exams (with Compliance)
Employment, IP, and vendor contracts for the management company

workflows.name: Regulatory filings production
workflows.description: Data gathered from Fund Accounting (AUM/RAUM, balance sheet), Risk (exposure/liquidity metrics), Ops (counterparty data); filings prepared, reviewed, senior-officer signed, submitted; evidence archived.
workflows.inputs: Fund accounting data, risk metrics, position data, prior filings
workflows.outputs: Submitted filings (ADV, PF, 13F, Annex IV, CPO-PQR), filing evidence archive

workflows.name: Trigger-event monitoring
workflows.description: Monitor ownership thresholds (13D/G within compressed deadlines), Form PF current-report events (e.g., extraordinary losses, margin/default events at qualifying hedge funds), short-position and derivatives reporting regimes across jurisdictions.
workflows.inputs: Position feeds, threshold tables from Compliance, market events
workflows.outputs: Event filings, breach memos if late

workflows.name: Fund/counterparty documentation
workflows.description: Negotiate and maintain ISDA/CSA/PB terms with Treasury; launch documents for new funds/share classes; maintain side-letter obligations database with IR.
workflows.inputs: Business terms, counterparty drafts, regulatory constraints
workflows.outputs: Executed agreements, terms databases, obligations registers

controls: Filings calendar with named owners and secondary reviewers (PRACTICE)
Attestation/sign-off chain for each filing (PRACTICE; SM&CR makes a named senior manager personally accountable in the UK)
Data lineage from books of record to filed numbers (PRACTICE; Form PF data quality is an SEC exam focus)
departments.independence: independent control/shared service reporting to GC/COO.

handoffs.content: Shared regulatory change management; exam and inquiry coordination
Executed ISDA/PB terms governing margin and financing mechanics
Side letter terms to be operationalized and monitored

regulatoryBasis: Form PF (SEC/CFTC joint rule; 2024 amendments — compliance dates extended in 2025 and further rulemaking in 2026; status should be re-verified at implementation time)
Form ADV (Advisers Act Rule 204-1)
Exchange Act 13(d)/13(g)/13(f) reporting
AIFMD Annex IV (EU-marketed funds); CFTC Part 4 (CPO-PQR) where registered
practiceNotes: VARIATION: Where compliance vs legal owns each filing differs by firm; what matters is a single calendar with unambiguous ownership.

PRACTICE: Regulatory reporting data should be sourced from the same certified books of record as investor reporting — divergent numbers across Form PF, ADV, and investor letters is a classic exam finding.

departments.name: Internal Audit (Third Line)
departments.mandate: Provide independent, objective assurance to the board/audit committee that the first and second lines' risk management and controls are designed and operating effectively across the entire lifecycle.
responsibilities: Risk-based annual audit plan covering investment process, valuation, ops, compliance program, technology, and vendor oversight
Execution of audits with documented findings and ratings
Remediation tracking and validation of closure
Reporting to board/audit committee, functionally independent of management

workflows.name: Audit planning
workflows.description: Risk-assess the audit universe; plan approved by audit committee; coordinated with external audit and compliance testing to avoid gaps/overlap.
workflows.inputs: Risk assessments, incident/breach registers, prior findings, regulatory developments
workflows.outputs: Approved audit plan

workflows.name: Audit execution and reporting
workflows.cadence: Per plan (e.g., 6-12 audits/year at a large manager)
workflows.description: Fieldwork, findings with root cause and management action plans, ratings, issuance; escalation of overdue remediation.
workflows.inputs: Process documentation, samples, system evidence
workflows.outputs: Audit reports, findings register, remediation tracker, audit committee pack

controls: Functional reporting line to board/audit committee, administrative to CEO/COO (PRACTICE per IIA Three Lines Model)
Audit charter with unrestricted access rights (PRACTICE)
QA and independence safeguards (no auditing of areas recently managed) (PRACTICE)
departments.independence: fully independent (third line) — the only function whose independence is from both the business AND the second line.

handoffs.content: Assurance opinions, thematic findings, remediation status
Outbound: findings and action plans. Inbound: evidence, remediation submissions

regulatoryBasis: IIA Three Lines Model (industry framework, not law for private advisers)
No US rule requires private fund managers to maintain internal audit; UK/EU expectations arise via SYSC proportionality for larger firms
practiceNotes: MAJOR VARIATION: Most hedge funds below ~$10bn have no standalone internal audit function — they rely on co-sourced/outsourced internal audit, mock SEC exams, SOC 1 reports from administrators, and the annual financial audit. Large multi-strats and traditional asset managers run full in-house functions. An operating model should treat IA as a scalable/outsourceable third line, not assume in-house.

stageGates.gate: PM/desk triage of one-pager; quick-kill criteria
stageGates.controlPoint: Idea provenance logged; MNPI check before expert access

stageGates.gate: Memo completeness standard (thesis, catalysts, invalidation levels, downside)
IC approval above thresholds OR limits-based autonomy within risk budget (firm-dependent)
stageGates.controlPoint: IC minutes with conditions; risk pre-read
stageGates.keyArtifact: IC decision record / approved size band

stageGates.gate: Mandate/limit headroom check; pre-trade allocation statement for aggregated orders
Pre-trade compliance gate (restricted list, thresholds, locates)

stageGates.controlPoint: Exception queue worked T+0; SoD between trading and settlement

stageGates.gate: Daily position/cash reconciliation clean or breaks explained

stageGates.controlPoint: Daily limit monitoring; breach escalation with cure windows; automatic capital cuts at pod platforms

stageGates.gate: Month-end close: administrator NAV vs shadow books reconciled; valuation committee approval of fair-valued positions and overrides
stageGates.controlPoint: Pricing hierarchy; independent override review (IOSCO principle)

stageGates.gate: Official returns from accounting book of record only
stageGates.controlPoint: Independent performance calculation; GIPS composite rules where claimed
stageGates.keyArtifact: Performance book of record + attribution reports

stageGates.gate: Compliance review of all investor-facing materials; filing sign-off chain
stageGates.controlPoint: Marketing Rule review; single data lineage to filings
stageGates.keyArtifact: Approved investor letter; filed Form PF/ADV/13F evidence

stageGates.gate: Thesis invalidation / stale-thesis / drawdown triggers force formal review

structuralRecommendations: Keep Risk and Compliance as two separate, fully independent second-line departments — they have different skill sets, cadences, and regulatory anchors (AIFMD Art. 15 vs Rule 206(4)-7); merging them is only defensible at very small scale.

Model the Investment Committee as a governance forum (workflow + artifacts), not a department — and make it optional/configurable, since multi-strategy pod platforms replace per-idea IC gates with central-risk limit enforcement.

Split Middle Office (trade ops/recon) from Fund Accounting & Valuation — they have different clocks (T+0/T+1 vs month-end), different counterparties (brokers vs administrator/auditor), and the valuation committee needs its own independence structure.

Add Treasury/Financing & Collateral as a distinct function for a multi-strategy context — margin, financing, and counterparty management is a first-order survival discipline at levered multi-strats and is materially under-modeled if folded into 'operations'.

House model validation separately from quant development (even as a small team or external arrangement) per SR 11-7-lineage independence expectations; tier the regime by model materiality per the 2026 revised interagency guidance.

Keep Performance & Attribution independent of the front office and downstream of Fund Accounting — its outputs feed both IC capital allocation and compliance-reviewed marketing, so it must be a single source of truth.
Treat Internal Audit as a scalable third line that may be outsourced/co-sourced below large-manager scale rather than a mandatory in-house department.
Give Legal & Regulatory Reporting a single jointly-owned filings calendar with Compliance rather than duplicating regulatory functions across both.
disagreementsAndVariation: IC gating vs limits-based autonomy: single-strategy/concentrated funds and institutional long-only managers gate ideas through investment committees; multi-strategy pod platforms deliberately do not, relying on hard central risk limits, drawdown-triggered capital cuts, and capital reallocation. Sources describing 'IC best practice' (Partners Capital, Cambridge Associates) are written for allocators/asset owners and transfer only partially to pod platforms.
Trading organization: centralized dealing desk (better best-ex evidence, information barriers) vs pod-embedded execution (speed, specialization) — both exist at scale; a platform must support both.

Internal audit presence: near-universal at banks and large traditional managers; frequently absent or outsourced at hedge funds — the three-lines model is aspirational, not descriptive, for much of the hedge fund industry.
GIPS: voluntary; near-standard for institutional long-only managers, materially less adopted by hedge funds. Claiming it creates firm-wide obligations (composites for ALL strategies).

Model risk management: SR 11-7 is bank guidance, not buy-side law; its buy-side application is a widely used benchmark but the depth applied is a judgment call. Note the April 2026 revised interagency guidance (OCC Bulletin 2026-13 / Fed SR letter) replaced the 2011 framework with a risk-based approach and scoped out generative/agentic AI — buy-side practice references should cite the lineage, not treat 2011 text as current.

Form PF: the 2024 amendments' compliance dates were extended multiple times (Feb 2025, Sep 2025) with further rulemaking in April 2026 — the exact current reporting content should be re-verified at build time rather than hard-coded.

SEC Private Fund Advisers rules (2023): vacated by the Fifth Circuit (June 2024). Quarterly statements, mandatory adviser-led audits, and restricted-activities provisions from that rulemaking are NOT current requirements, though allocators demand much of it contractually — a common source of confusion in secondary literature.

Valuation model: AIFMD permits either a functionally independent internal valuation function or an external valuer; US practice relies on administrator NAV + manager valuation committee. The administrator is not always a true independent valuer for Level 3 assets — third-party valuation agents fill that gap; how far to go is firm judgment.
Risk reporting line: CRO to CEO/board (strong form) vs to COO/CIO (weak form, common at small funds) — allocator ODD treats the weak form as a finding; no US rule prohibits it.

Best execution reporting: EU/UK RTS 27/28 public reports were rolled back, but the underlying best-execution and monitoring obligations remain; US advisers have no prescriptive rule but a fiduciary duty with an enforcement track record — the control (quarterly best-ex committee) is convergent practice across regimes despite divergent rules.

sourcesConsulted.whatItSupported: Rule 206(4)-7 compliance program requirements: written P&Ps, annual review, designated CCO
Rule 204-2 books-and-records requirements incl. order memoranda (who recommended, who placed, terms) and 5-year retention
SEC April 2022 exams risk alert on MNPI/204A deficiencies: restricted list add/remove discipline, expert network controls, access-person requirements under 204A-1
FINRA Rule 5310 best execution text (reasonable diligence, best market)
FINRA 'regular and rigorous' execution-quality review expectation; best-ex committee documentation practice
SEC aggregation/allocation practice: pre-trade allocation statements, pro-rata partial fills, average pricing, prohibition on performance/fee-driven allocation
Form PF 2024 amendments; large hedge fund adviser quarterly filing within 60 days; qualifying hedge fund reporting
Form PF compliance-date extensions — basis for flagging that Form PF status must be re-verified at build time
Form PF structure: sections, current reports for large hedge fund advisers, risk/exposure content
Evidence of the Private Fund Advisers rules vacatur — reverted rule text published by SEC (quarterly statement/audit mandates not in force)
IOSCO Principles for the Valuation of Hedge Fund Portfolios (2007): nine principles, independent oversight of valuation policy, price-override review by an independent party
GIPS 2020 for Firms: firm-wide compliance, composite completeness, fair-value requirement
GIPS verification: firm-wide only, assurance on policies/procedures design and implementation; best practice not requirement

Revised interagency model risk management guidance (April 17, 2026; OCC/Fed/FDIC) replacing 2011 SR 11-7/OCC 2011-12 framework: risk-based tailoring, non-prescriptive, generative/agentic AI scoped out
Federal Reserve issuance of the revised model risk management guidance (SR letter superseding SR 11-7 lineage) — PDF fetched; content cross-confirmed via OCC bulletin
FCA SYSC 24: allocation of prescribed senior-management responsibilities under SM&CR
SM&CR guide: SMF16 compliance oversight, Duty of Responsibility for senior managers
AIMA Guides to Sound Practices as the industry sound-practice corpus (valuation, investor relations, due diligence)
AIMA DDQ as the industry-standard manager due diligence template (governance, investment process, ops, service providers)
AIMA Guide to Sound Practices for the Valuation of Investments (2018 edition) — manager-side valuation governance practice
MFA Sound Practices for Hedge Fund Managers: compliance culture, valuation, risk management, BCP/DR, trading and business operations framework
Independent NAV validation / shadow NAV expectations from investors
Shadow accounting practice: parallel books, month-end trial balance reconciliation with administrator
CFA Institute Research Foundation (Bacon) attribution literature: Brinson-Hood-Beebower allocation/selection, factor-based attribution distinctions
Investment committee best practice: governance-not-investing role, policy compliance oversight, documentation discipline
Institutional governance foundations for investment committees (asset-owner perspective)
Three Lines of Defense model: first-line risk ownership, second-line independent oversight (risk/compliance), third-line internal audit reporting to board/audit committee
Multi-strategy pod platform risk architecture: pod risk budgets, drawdown-triggered capital cuts, centralized real-time risk
Regulatory examination expectations for best-execution monitoring programs
Front/middle/back office division of labor at hedge funds (trade support, recon, settlement, fund accounting)

confidenceNotes: High confidence on US regulatory anchors (206(4)-7, 204A-1, 204-2, custody rule, Form PF mechanics, FINRA 5310), GIPS, IOSCO valuation principles, and the three-lines/independence architecture — all tied to primary or near-primary sources. Medium confidence on: exact current Form PF content (compliance dates shifted through 2025-2026), the precise contents of the April 2026 revised model-risk guidance (bulletin summary consulted, full text not parsed), and EU/UK best-execution reporting rollback details (well-known but not re-verified here). Multi-strat pod risk-limit specifics (5%/7.5% drawdown conventions) come from credible industry commentary, not primary sources — treat as illustrative, not normative.
